thief: Getting arrested and going to prision for doing a robbery
Doing a robbery and not being caught and getting away with it
Applying for a job and being rejected after a background check reveals record of being a thief
Being turned down for housing because of a criminal record despite having income and references
Attending a mandatory check in with a probation officer as a regular feature of weekly life
Violating a parole condition unintentionally and facing the possibility of being sent back to prison
Being unable to travel freely due to parole or probation restrictions on movement
Paying court ordered fines or restitution from a very limited income each month
Rebuilding an identification document — ID, passport, license — that was lost or expired due to criminal records
Telling a potential employer about a criminal record before a background check reveals it and not getting the job
Being monitored by an electronic ankle bracelet as a daily visible reminder of legal supervision
Being stopped by police in daily life and experiencing the fear
Trying to reconnect with family members who are uncertain, cautious, or unwilling to talk because of being a thief
Experiencing a family member cutting off contact because of the criminal past and managing that loss
Rebuilding trust with a partner, parent, or sibling after the damage caused by past criminal activity
Living in a halfway house or supervised accommodation as a condition of release
Being unable to vote, hold certain licenses, or access certain public services due to a criminal conviction
Experiencing shame when meeting new people who do not know the criminal history and managing what to disclose
Being recognized in a community by someone who knows the criminal history and experiencing the social weight of that
Attending court for an ongoing legal matter
Meeting someone else who has been through the criminal justice system
Beginning to build a daily life — routine, work, relationships, stability — that makes returning to old patterns feel less necessary
Beggar: Sitting in a public space asking passersby for money and experiencing the hours of being ignored or dismissed
Experiencing the specific humiliation of being looked through by people who avoid eye contact while passing because you are a beggar
Receiving a small amount of money from a stranger and experiencing the specific relief that even a small amount produces
Being moved on by security or police because you are a beggar from a spot where regular money was being received
Finding a new location to beg after being removed from a previous spot and starting the process over
Experiencing the physical exposure of spending long hours outside in heat, cold, rain, or wind
Receiving food instead of money from a passerby and being grateful despite needing money more
Being verbally abused or mocked by someone passing while asking for help
Experiencing a moment of genuine human connection with a stranger who stops, gives, and speaks with respect
Being robbed of the money collected during a day of begging by someone
Receiving a meal from a soup kitchen, food bank, or charitable organization as the primary daily food source
Experiencing illness — cold, infection, injury — without access to medical care or a place to recover
Having personal belongings stolen or destroyed while sleeping rough
in_prison: Being arrested and taken into custody by police
Spending time in a holding cell or jail before trial or sentencing
Being sentenced to prison and experiencing the shock of that moment
Working in a prison job — kitchen, laundry, maintenance — and experiencing the routine and purpose that provides
Attending educational or vocational programs offered in prison and experiencing the opportunity for growth they provide
Maintaining contact with family and friends outside through visits, phone calls, or letters while in prison
Experiencing the emotional toll of separation from loved ones while incarcerated
Facing disciplinary actions for rule violations within the prison system and navigating their consequences
Participating in rehabilitation programs for substance abuse or mental health issues while in prison
Experiencing the specific challenges of reentry planning while still incarcerated — finding housing, employment, support networks for release
Being released from prison and experiencing the shock and adjustment of that moment
Being released from prison and navigating the challenges of reentry into society — finding housing, employment, rebuilding relationships, managing legal obligations
pregnant: Finding out about the pregnancy and experiencing the initial shock and emotions of that moment
Attending regular prenatal appointments and experiencing the physical and emotional changes of pregnancy
Experiencing morning sickness, fatigue, and other common symptoms of pregnancy on a daily basis
Making decisions about prenatal care, nutrition, and lifestyle changes to support a healthy pregnancy
Navigating the social dynamics of pregnancy
Preparing for childbirth by attending classes, creating a birth plan, and gathering necessary supplies
Experiencing the physical discomforts of late pregnancy — back pain, swelling, difficulty sleeping — on a daily basis
Going into labor and experiencing the intensity and unpredictability of that process
Giving birth and experiencing the physical and emotional aftermath of that moment
Recovering from childbirth while caring for a newborn and navigating the challenges of new parenthood
having a miscarriage and experiencing the grief and loss that comes with that
Experiencing complications during pregnancy or childbirth and navigating the medical interventions and emotional toll that produces
Balancing work, family, and pregnancy-related responsibilities and challenges on a daily basis
